segments.source_text: CIPS Translation Pipeline
Proof of concept — summary report
Internal

Contents
Executive summary

This report summarises a proof of concept for an AI-powered document translation pipeline for CIPS (Chartered Institute of Procurement & Supply) Corporate Award Practitioner training materials. The objective was to test whether PPTX and DOCX training content could be translated automatically from English into other languages, preserving formatting, with minimal manual intervention, and with a design that could plausibly extend to ten to fifteen languages at enterprise scale.

The work described here is the proof of concept, not a finished enterprise solution. It was carried out by a single developer using personal credentials and web-based tooling, with the explicit goal of establishing whether the underlying approach was sound before any investment in a production build. On that basis, the proof of concept succeeded: a 99-slide PowerPoint deck was translated into French at a 99.02% automated handle rate, and a Word document handout at 100%, both through a single, repeatable, end-to-end automated flow. The sections below set out the problem the proof of concept was testing, the approaches trialled and discarded along the way, the approach that was ultimately validated, and what would need to happen next to move from proof of concept to an enterprise-ready service.
The initial problem

CIPS needed to establish whether a substantial volume of existing training material (PowerPoint decks and Word documents) could realistically be translated into multiple languages without a translation effort that grew in a linear, and eventually prohibitive, way with each additional document or language. Before committing to a production build, the proof of concept set out to test whether an approach could:
Preserve the original formatting, layout, and slide structure exactly, so that translated materials look and behave like the English originals.
Avoid a process that required manual copy-paste, manual re-formatting, or manual reconciliation of translated text back into the source file for every document and every language.

Plausibly scale from a single pilot language (French, FR-FR) towards ten to fifteen languages, including languages using non-Latin scripts, without the effort per language or per document growing in a way that would make the process impractical at volume.
Maintain consistent, approved terminology across all procurement and supply-chain specific language, rather than relying on ad hoc or inconsistent translation of technical terms.
Provide a quality assurance step and a clear record of translation decisions, so that subject-matter experts could review and sign off before materials were finalised.

The proof of concept was built and run by a single developer, working entirely through web-based interfaces (GitHub, Cloudflare, Optimizely Opal) on personal accounts, with no terminal or local code execution environment. This scope, one developer, no dedicated infrastructure, is an important part of the context for the outcomes reported here: the proof of concept was designed to test the underlying approach as cheaply and quickly as possible, not to deliver production infrastructure.
Approaches trialled during the proof of concept

Several designs were tried and discarded in the course of the proof of concept, each because it failed to hold up once tested against a real document at realistic scale. These are recorded here because they informed the approach that was ultimately validated, and because they identify constraints that a future production build should not repeat.
Fully agent-driven extraction (discarded)

The earliest design used a single AI agent to open, read and transcribe the source PPTX or DOCX file directly, converting its content into structured JSON for the rest of the pipeline. Testing showed that large language models were not reliable at reading binary Office file formats: they hallucinated text, missed content, and could not guarantee that the extracted text matched the source precisely enough for automated reconstruction. This was identified early as a risk to the whole approach, since any mismatch between extracted and actual source text would prevent the reconstruction step from finding the right place to insert a translation.

This finding shaped a working principle carried through the rest of the proof of concept: language models should not be relied upon to read or write binary file formats directly. Deterministic code should do that job instead (see section 4).
An AI agent committing translated batches to GitHub (discarded)

An intermediate design used a dedicated AI agent to commit each batch of translated text back to GitHub as a JSON file. This worked for small batches but failed intermittently for larger ones. The cause was that the agent's only available web-browsing tool issued requests with the file content encoded directly into the URL, and URLs longer than roughly 2 kilobytes were being silently truncated, corrupting or losing content for batches larger than around three text segments.

This was resolved by removing that agent from the workflow and having the translation agent commit its own output directly, using a purpose-built tool that sends the full content in the request body rather than the URL. This removed the size ceiling and was carried forward into the validated approach.
Index-based distribution of translated lines into slide paragraphs (discarded)

An early version of the reconstruction logic distributed translated lines into a PowerPoint shape's paragraphs purely by position. Many slide shapes contain empty spacer paragraphs used for visual formatting between bullet points. Distributing by raw position caused translated content to be written into these empty spacer paragraphs, where it was silently discarded, while the paragraphs that should have held the text were left blank. This was replaced with mapping that identifies non-empty source paragraphs and places translated lines only onto those.
Treating all extracted segments as text-box content (discarded)

An early version of the matching logic allowed short, non-text-box segments, such as labels embedded in logos or images, into the same matching process as ordinary text. Because these segments were often short strings, they occasionally matched the wrong shape, overwriting unrelated text elsewhere on the slide. This was corrected by explicitly excluding non-text-box segments from the matching loop.
The approach validated by the proof of concept

The approach that emerged from this trial-and-error process, and that was used to produce the results in section 5, is a hybrid pipeline. Deterministic Python scripts handle everything requiring exact fidelity to the source file (reading and writing PPTX/DOCX structure), while a sequence of four AI agents handles the parts of the task that require language understanding: terminology matching, translation, and quality review. For the proof of concept, this ran across two personal GitHub repositories, a Cloudflare Pages front end, Cloudflare R2 storage, and a Cloudflare Worker bridging the AI workflow tool (Optimizely Opal) to GitHub. This is presented here as the approach proven to work at proof-of-concept scale, not as a description of finished production infrastructure.
End-to-end flow tested
A user uploads a source PPTX or DOCX file and selects a target locale via a simple web page (Cloudflare Pages).
The file is uploaded to Cloudflare R2 object storage, and a GitHub Actions workflow (extract.yml) is triggered automatically.

extract.yml runs a Python script that uses the python-pptx and python-docx libraries to read the file directly and produce structured batches of text segments as JSON, in fixed batches of 20 segments.
The extraction step commits these batches to the repository and automatically fires a webhook into the Optimizely Opal AI workflow.

Within Opal, each batch passes through a loop of three further agents: a terminology-scanning agent, a translation agent, and a quality assurance agent, each drawing on shared reference files for approved terminology and locale-specific rules.

The translation agent commits its own output directly back to GitHub using a custom tool that posts the full JSON payload to the Cloudflare Worker, avoiding the URL length restriction described in section 3.2.

Once all batches for a document are processed, a merge step consolidates them into a single translated JSON file, and a reconstruction step writes the translated text back into a copy of the original PPTX or DOCX file, preserving formatting.

The finished, translated document is uploaded to Cloudflare R2 with a time-limited download link, and a status record is written back to the repository so the front end can display progress and provide the download.
Deterministic extraction and reconstruction

Extraction and reconstruction were handled entirely by Python scripts rather than by AI agents, guaranteeing that the text read from the source file, and the text written back into it, matched exactly. Reconstruction used a multi-tier matching strategy, trying an exact match first, then progressively looser matching (page-number-tolerant, substring, per-paragraph, and finally matching against the slide layout or slide master), so that the great majority of segments were placed automatically and only a small residue required manual attention.
Multi-tier terminology control

Approved procurement and supply-chain terminology was maintained centrally in a global terminology reference file, with locale-specific override files (FR-FR, for the pilot) layered on top for language-specific conventions such as register, gender agreement, and preferred renderings of standard phrases. This tested the principle of a single source of truth for terminology, intended to extend to further languages without altering the underlying agent logic.
Direct AI-to-GitHub integration

A Cloudflare Worker acted as a bridge between the Opal agents and the GitHub API, in two modes: a GET-based mode compatible with Opal's built-in web-browsing tool, used for triggering workflows and reading files; and a POST-based custom tool, used by the translation agent to commit its own output directly with the full payload in the request body.
Automated quality assurance

A fourth agent reviewed each translated batch against the same terminology references, checking for issues such as violation of a mandated term, mismatched line counts, or unresolved flagged terms, and routed the batch either straight through to reconstruction or to a queue for subject-matter expert review. Non-blocking issues, such as text becoming longer than the original in translation, were recorded for information rather than halting the pipeline, since expansion is a layout concern for reconstruction rather than a translation error.
Delivery and status reporting

Once a document was translated and reconstructed, it was stored in Cloudflare R2 with a seven-day presigned download link, and a status record was committed to the repository. The web front end read this status record to show the requester the outcome, including the automated handle rate achieved, and a download link, with a warning as the link approached expiry.
Outcomes of the proof of concept
The proof of concept produced two proven end-to-end results, each run through the full automated flow described in section 4 without manual intervention beyond the initial upload:
On the strength of these results, the proof of concept demonstrated the following, at the scale of a single developer and two test documents:
Upload-to-delivery can be fully automated: a user can upload a source file and locale and receive a translated document and a quality report without manually triggering individual pipeline steps.
Terminology can be centrally controlled and consistently applied across all agents in the workflow.
Large translation batches can be committed reliably once the URL-length ceiling identified in section 3.2 is removed.

The reconstruction approach can correctly preserve formatting, including edge cases such as spacer paragraphs and multi-line segments, once the failure modes identified in sections 3.3 and 3.4 are designed around.

The architecture can support both PowerPoint and Word documents with a shared agent workflow and a per-format reconstruction script, suggesting the design could extend to further formats without a fundamental redesign.

The residual, unresolved 0.98% of the pilot PPTX deck consists of text embedded within SmartArt graphics and images, which the automated pipeline cannot currently edit. This is expected to remain a manual, or content-redesign, item rather than something the pipeline itself can resolve, and should be treated as a known limitation of the approach rather than a defect to be engineered away.

It should be emphasised that these results confirm the proof of concept, not a production-ready service. They were achieved on two documents, in one target language, run by the person who built the pipeline, on personal infrastructure. Section 6 sets out what would be required to move from this point to an enterprise-ready solution.
From proof of concept to an enterprise-ready solution

The proof of concept was scoped to answer a single question: is this approach viable? Having established that it is, a distinct body of work remains before the pipeline could be considered enterprise-ready. This is not a list of minor refinements to the proof of concept; it represents the gap between a validated approach and a supported, scalable production service.
Making quality assurance visible outside the AI platform

Quality assurance findings are currently only visible within Optimizely Opal, which limits who can review them. The next step is to have the QA agent commit its findings to GitHub so they can be folded into the correction report that reviewers use, removing the dependency on Opal access.
Removing remaining reliability constraints

The Cloudflare Worker bridge should be registered as a full custom tool within Opal, so that every step currently relying on the browser-style web tool can move to the more robust direct integration used by the translation agent, removing the last remaining size and reliability constraints identified during the proof of concept.
Multi-document and multi-language operation

The upload interface and status page currently handle one file and show only the most recently completed job. An enterprise-ready service would need to handle batches of files and a full course's worth of modules as a single submission, with a status view covering every file and its outcome.
Format and language coverage

The proof of concept covered PPTX and DOCX in one language (FR-FR). Extending to PDF, HTML and SCORM source formats, and to the full target list of ten to fifteen languages, including non-Latin scripts, is untested work that will surface its own failure modes, in the same way the approaches in section 3 did for the first two formats and the first language. Non-Latin scripts in particular will need dedicated investigation into font availability, right-to-left layout handling, and quality checking that cannot rely on straightforward text comparison.
Infrastructure, ownership and support

The proof of concept ran on one developer's personal GitHub and Cloudflare accounts, with no shared ownership, access control, monitoring, or support model. Moving to an enterprise-ready solution requires migration to an organisational account, a defined process for maintaining terminology and locale files as more languages are added, and a support and monitoring model that does not depend on a single individual.
Testing and regression coverage

The proof of concept was validated against two hand-picked documents. An enterprise-ready solution would need a repeatable test suite covering a representative range of document structures, plus automated regression testing of the reconstruction logic, to give confidence that the pipeline continues to work correctly as it is extended to new formats and languages.
Conclusion

The proof of concept has answered the question it was set up to answer: an AI-assisted translation pipeline combining deterministic extraction and reconstruction with a multi-agent translation and QA workflow can automatically translate CIPS training materials at a very high handle rate, while preserving formatting, without manual reconciliation of every translated document. The approach that achieved this, described in section 4, was reached only after several less workable designs, described in section 3, were tried and ruled out on real documents. What has not yet been tested is the approach at enterprise scale: multiple document formats, multiple languages including non-Latin scripts, shared organisational ownership, and a supported production service. Section 6 sets out that remaining body of work.
